coredata.dc:title: Full-color emissive organosilicon-coated carbon dots microspheres for latent fingerprint development with level 3 details
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jiashi
Cao, Xiyue
Fan, Wen
Ma, Huijuan
Jiang, Xuanfeng
Sun, Zhengguang
Zhan, Yuan
coredata.dc:description: Organosilicon-coated carbon dots (OSiCDs) microspheres were synthesized by one-step solvothermal method in which phenylenediamine-derived CDs were encapsulated by organosilicon network structure to achieve blue, yellow and red emissive solid-state fluorescent powder. The influence of the three isomers of phenylenediamine on the solid-state fluorescence of OSiCDs microspheres were characterized in detail. Furthermore, the fluorescence stability of three kinds of OSiCDs microspheres were measured and exhibited excellent stability, which facilitate the acquisition and preservation of fingerprint patterns. The OSiCDs microspheres used as a fluorescence developer were further applied to capture the latent fingerprints (LFPs) on different substrate surfaces. The results indicate that the OSiCDs microspheres can efficiently eliminate background interferences of challenging surfaces, such as multicolored or patterned substrates, and the identified LFPs have clear patterns with high-resolution fluorescence, enabling fluorescent fingerprint recognition at three levels (core, hole, island, and fork). The as-prepared OSiCDs microspheres show great promise in practical application scenarios for fingerprint development.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Full-color emission
Organosilicon composites
Latent fingerprints recognition

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis route of the OSiCDs microspheres. UV–Vis, excitation, and PL emission spectra of (b) m-SiCDs, (c) o-SiCDs, and (d) p-SiCDs (Inset: m-SiCDs, o-SiCDs, and p-SiCDs photographs under daylight and a 365 nm light).
(a–c) FESEM images and (d–f) HRTEM image of m-SiCDs, o-SiCDs, and p-SiCDs (Inset: the lattice fringe distances of CDs in OSiCDs microspheres).

(a) Image of OSiCDs composites before and after etching under day light and 365 nm UV light (Each group of cuvettes are m-SiCDs, o-SiCDs, and p-SiCDs from left to right). (b–d) The PL emission spectra of OSiCDs composites after etching by NaOH solution under different excitation wavelengths.
Relative PL intensity of OSiCDs microspheres treated (a) at temperatures from 30 to 130 °C, (b) in various organic solvents, and (c) under different durations of UV light radiation.
The level 1–3 characteristics collected by OSiCDs microspheres on aluminized paper.
Fingerprints developed by OSiCDs microspheres on several typical surfaces and captured by an iPhone under UV excitation (dashed line: “scar”; solid line: “bifurcation”).
Level 1–3 fingerprints developed using OSiCDs microspheres on glass at different times and captured by using stereoscopic microscope.
Images of LFPs aged at 80 °C after 30 days and then captured by OSiCDs microspheres (Shooting tool: iPhone 13).

head.ce:title: Full-color emissive organosilicon-coated carbon dots microspheres for latent fingerprint development with level 3 details

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Three Gorges Laboratory, Yichang, 443000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Three Gorges Laboratory, Yichang 443000, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan, 430062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials
Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Hubei University, Wuhan, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organosilicon-coated carbon dots (OSiCDs) microspheres were synthesized by one-step solvothermal method in which phenylenediamine-derived CDs were encapsulated by organosilicon network structure to achieve blue, yellow and red emissive solid-state fluorescent powder. The influence of the three isomers of phenylenediamine on the solid-state fluorescence of OSiCDs microspheres were characterized in detail. Furthermore, the fluorescence stability of three kinds of OSiCDs microspheres were measured and exhibited excellent stability, which facilitate the acquisition and preservation of fingerprint patterns. The OSiCDs microspheres used as a fluorescence developer were further applied to capture the latent fingerprints (LFPs) on different substrate surfaces. The results indicate that the OSiCDs microspheres can efficiently eliminate background interferences of challenging surfaces, such as multicolored or patterned substrates, and the identified LFPs have clear patterns with high-resolution fluorescence, enabling fluorescent fingerprint recognition at three levels (core, hole, island, and fork). The as-prepared OSiCDs microspheres show great promise in practical application scenarios for fingerprint development.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fingerprint is regarded as important evidence of personal identity because of its uniqueness, invariance and difficult disguise [1,2]. Latent fingerprints (LFPs) are formed by natural secretions of the skin left on the surface, which are often invisible to the naked eye [3]. To achieve the visualization of LFPs, a developer is usually needed to tightly bound the skin secretions through physical or chemical interactions. The fingerprint pattern should strongly contrast with the substrate background to become clearly visible, which requires the fingerprint developer to have the characteristics of high contrast, high selectivity, high sensitivity, and low toxicity. Therefore, the optical property of developer has a decisive influence on the development effect. However, the most existing fluorescent materials still have some shortcomings [4]. For instance, some organic fluorophores have weaker fluorescence or shorter emission wavelengths, which results in low-contrast LFP images, while the better-performing organic fluorophores often need complex molecular structures, making them costly in practical applications [5]. Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) materials often involve using organic solvents in their long-time preparation and fingerprint identification process [6]. Semiconductor quantum dots usually contain expensive metals or toxic heavy metal ions [7]. These shortcomings greatly hinder the wide application of traditional fingerprint developers in fingerprint recognition. Thus, it is necessary to explore a series of different fingerprint developers with novel characteristics such as environmental friendliness, suitable size and morphology, and good adsorption.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attractive properties such as excellent optical properties, cost-effectiveness, and low toxicity, making them have important application potential in the field of fingerprint recognition [8–12]. The fluorescence intensity of most CDs is significantly reduced or even quenched after drying due to the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching effect. Therefore, CDs are usually dispersed in solvents (organic solvents, hydrochloric acid, etc.) to develop fingerprints by the spraying method. However, these solvents are usually corrosive to plastics, leather etc. And are not suitable to detect LFPs on surface with high water absorbing property such as paper, which greatly limits the application of CDs in fingerprint identification [13,14]. Organosilicon, as a typical organic-inorganic hybrid material, is not only easy to control in terms of structure and morphology, but also has low preparation cost, good stability, environmental friendliness, and easy functionalization of the surface, making it an ideal substrate for designing and preparing solid-state emissive CDs composites [15–17]. For example, Sun et al. innovated an ingenious strategy of encapsulating silane-functionalized CDs (Si-CDs) in amorphous silica using biowaste [18]. The obtained Si-CDs@SiO2 composite exhibit time-dependent room temperature phosphorite (RTP) colors and excitation-dependent RTP colors. And the Si-CDs@SiO2 powders as the developer could be well observed and differentiated the fingerprints details, especially when the UV light is turned off. Our team prepared solid-state emissive CDs-organosilicon (CDs-OSi) composites with different morphologies for LFPs identification, and it find that the CDs-OSi composite with particle size of 2 μm has the most suitable size and dispersion, which exhibits excellent fingerprint recognition capability [19]. However, CDs-OSi composites have a limited range of fluorescence emission wavelength regulation, mainly in the blue and green light regions, limiting the contrast of fingerprint development patterns because of the blue fluorescence interference produced by most substrates themselves. To obtain clear recognition, there must be a contrast between the substrate background and the fingerprint pattern formed by CDs [20]. Therefore, it is necessary to prepare fingerprint developers with suitable particle size, high dispersity, regular morphology, and wider fluorescence emission wavelength [21].

Herein, three kinds of organosilicon-coated carbon dots (OSiCDs) composites with blue, yellow, and red fluorescence were synthesized from isomers of phenylenediamine and 3-ureidopropyl triethoxy silane (UPTES) as the precursors by the one-step solvothermal method. In particular, OSiCDs microspheres have a regular and dispersed spherical shape with a suitable particle size of 2 μm for fingerprint recognition. The OSiCDs microspheres also have good fluorescence stability even in extreme environments such as high temperature, long-term ultraviolet irradiation, and immersion in organic solvents. Further exploration was conducted on the fingerprint recognition performance of three OSiCDs microspheres on substrates with different colors and materials. The results show that all three kinds of OSiCDs microspheres can identify level 1–3 features on glass, aluminum foil, plastic, and other substrates. Furthermore, OSiCDs microspheres with different fluorescence can provide a variety of choices on different colored substrates to ensure the contrast and clarity of fingerprint development patterns. Even though the LFPs were aging treated at ventilation or high temperature, OSiCDs microspheres can still obtain clear fingerprint characteristics to meet the detail requirements of identity identification. The work innovates a strategy for the preparation of multicolor solid-state fluorescent organosilicon-coated CDs composites, and further broadens the application scenarios of CDs in LFPs recognition.

o-Phenylenediamine (o-PD, AR, 98 %), m-Phenylenediamine (m-PD, AR, 98 %), p-Phenylenediamine (p-PD, AR, 97 %), 3-ureidopropyl triethoxy organosilicon (UPTES, 40.0–50.0 % in methanol), and 1,2-dichlorobenzene (98 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Methanol (CP) and methylbenzene (≥99.5 %) were obtained from Hubei Aosheng New Material Technology Co., Ltd. Chlorobenzene (AR) was purchased from Tianjin Damao chemicals reagent factory. N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, AR, 99.5 %) were produced by Beijing InnoChem Science & Technology Co., Ltd. Bromobenzene (AR), Ethanol (EtOH, ≥99.7 %), acetone (AR), and tetrahydrofuran (THF, AR) were produced by Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd.

In the experiment, 0.85 mmol (0.09 g) m-PD, o-PD, and p-PD were dissolved in a mixed solvent consisting of 16 mL H2O and 4 mL EtOH, respectively. After adding 6 mmol (1.76 g) UPTES, the three kinds of solution were stirred for 10 min. Then the solution was reacted for 12 h at 180 °C in a thermostatic air blast drying oven to obtain the solid-liquid mixture. H2O and EtOH were used as detergents to clean the products three times in turn by centrifugation at 7500 r/min for 5 min to remove impurities. Finally, the OSiCDs microspheres were achieved after drying in a 50 °C oven. The composites prepared from m-PD, o-PD, and p-PD are called m-SiCDs, o-SiCDs, and p-SiCDs, respectively.

The index fingers of a male and female were used as samples to obtain LFPs in this experiment. First, the fingers gently rubbed the forehead or nose gently to adhere grease and sweat stains on the skin. Then, the fingers were pressed against the surfaces of the substrate, causing LFPs to be left behind. Second, OSiCDs powders were covered on LFPs by a goose-down stick doting, and the excess powders were blown off with a washing ear ball. At this point, fingerprints showed up. Finally, a stereo microscope and an iPhone 13 were used to take photographs of fingerprints, respectively.

Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) was performed to observe the morphology of OSiCDs microspheres under an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. The state of the CDs in the OSiCDs microspheres were measured in TMO Talos F200X high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were measured using a TMO Nicolet iS50 Spectrometer (400-4000 cm−1). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on a thermo scientific ESCAlab 250xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The C1s peak is used to calibrate the binding energy at 284.8 eV. The XPS spectra images were fitted using the Avantage software. The fluorescence spectra were collected by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PicoQuant Fluo Time 300). The fluorescence spectrophotometer with an integrating sphere was used to characteristic the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and the fluorescence lifetime curves on 420 nm laser. The UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained by a Shimadzu Corp UV-3600 spectrophotometer. Crystallographic information was collected by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) equipped with a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Cu ΚR radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å)).

Preliminary research works have reported that the CDs are embedded in organosilicon to achieve the composites that not only can display tunable fluorescence and controllable size, but also exhibit good affinity for fingerprint residues, which have great potential in the development and recognition of LFPs [22]. Our previous work discovered that the CDs embedded organosilicon (CDs-OSi) composites can bind well to the secretions of fingerprints. Level 2 and level 3 features of the fingerprint can be clearly identified, especially the composites have a particle size about 2 μm [15]. However, the CDs-OSi composites prepared with L-(−)-malic acid as sole carbon source emit fluorescence in the blue and green region, limiting the contrast of fingerprint development patterns due to blue fluorescence interference from most substrates themself. The structure and properties of CDs themselves have an important influence on the fluorescence properties of their composites [23]. Therefore, the three kinds isomers of phenylenediamine (m-PD, o-PD, and p-PD) were used as carbon source in turn to react with UPTES, and three kinds of OSiCDs microspheres (named as m-SiCDs, o-SiCDs and p-SiCDs) were obtained by the one-step solvothermal for 12 h at 180 °C (Fig. 1 a).

The optical properties of OSiCDs microspheres were explored in Fig. 1b–d. The UV–vis absorption spectra appear two absorption regions of three OSiCDs microspheres. The π-π∗ transitions of the C=C and C=N bonds in the aromatic ring structure is manifested in the absorption between 250 and 300 nm [24]. The n-π∗ transitions of C=O, C=C, and C=N determine the state of the OSiCDs microspheres in the low energy region [25]. Among them, the UV–vis spectrum of m-SiCDs shows a low-energy absorption tail band extending to longer wavelengths, o-SiCDs has a sharp absorption peak at about 437 nm, and p-SiCDs has a wide absorption band in the range of 385–535 nm. It is evident that the abundance of C=O, C=C, and C=N conjugate structures in m-SiCDs, o-SiCDs, and p-SiCDs increases in turn. The PL spectra show that m-SiCDs, o-SiCDs, and p-SiCDs exhibits blue (463 nm), yellow (531 nm) and red (595 nm) with their optimum excitation wavelength (Fig. 1b–d). The insets present their colors under 365 nm UV light, which concur with their spectra. It can be found that the different content of conjugated structures in m-SiCDs, o-SiCDs, and p-SiCDs leads to different absorption curves in the low energy region of UV–vis spectrum, and the richer the conjugated structures, the more obvious the emission peak redshift [26].

The morphology of OSiCDs microspheres was investigated by FESEM and HRTEM (Fig. 2 ). FESEM images show that the three OSiCDs microspheres have good monodispersity and perfect spherical morphologies with an average diameter about 2 μm (Fig. 2a–c). This is attributed that isomers of phenylenediamine dissolved in the solvent are conducive to forming an alkaline environment, which is able to promote polycondensation of UPTES to form the regular microspheres under the basic conditions [27]. As shown in Fig. 2d–f, CDs cannot be clearly observed in HRTEM images due to the organosilicon wrapped around CDs. But it can be seen that the lattice fringe distances of CDs in m-SiCDs are 0.19 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 0) plane of graphite, while those of o-SiCDs and p-SiCDs are both 0.26 nm, which are close to the graphite (0 2 0) diffraction plane, which is consistent with the content reported in previous reports [28,29]. XRD pattern presents a broad peak centered at 2θ = 21.39°, indicating the OSiCDs microspheres are amorphous (Fig. S1) [30].

FT-IR and XPS spectroscopy were applied to investigate the functional groups and elemental compositions of OSiCDs microspheres. The chemical compositions of three kinds of OSiCDs microspheres are similar in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S2). The analysis shows that the OSiCDs microspheres contain the typical stretching vibrations of N-H (3364 cm−1), C-H (2994 and 2873 cm−1), C=O (1630 cm−1), N-H (1567 cm−1) and C-N (1290 cm−1) [31]. The absorption peak at 1501 cm−1, attributes to stretching vibration of C=C of the aromatic ring [32–35]. The Si-O-Si and Si-O-C bonds correspond to the peaks centered at 1138 and 1032 cm−1, respectively [36]. Si-C bonds stretching vibrations are present from 814 to 650 cm−1 [37]. The FT-IR spectrum can confirm that a “Si-O-Si” and “Si-O-C″ network structure encloses CDs formed by phenylenediamine. Furthermore, the chemical composition of the OSiCDs microspheres was analyzed XPS in Fig. S3. Full-scan XPS spectra (Fig. S3a) reveal carbon(C), silicon (Si), oxygen (O), and nitrogen(N) elements belong to the OSiCDs microspheres. As the fluorescence color changes from blue to red, the content of C decrease, while the ratios of N and Si elements increase successively (Fig. S3b). This is because amine fragments enhance donor-acceptor mechanism of charge transfer, lowering the band gap of CDs composites or forming interband states [38]. Therefore, the redshift emission of OSiCDs microspheres can originate from increased nitrogen content in CDs. The C1s XPS spectra are deconvoluted into four peaks at C=O (288.14 eV), C-N (285.85 eV), C-Si (284.99 eV), and C-C (284.80 eV) (Figs. S3c–e) [39–41]. The N1s XPS spectra are mainly divided into N-H and N-C peaks around 399.99 and 400.10 eV, respectively (Figs. S3f–h) [42]. The Si-2p spectrum is divided into two peaks near 101.76 and 102.7 eV, belonging to Si-c and Si-o, respectively (Figs. S3i–k) [43]. The above results indicate that the three isomers of phenylenediamine as carbon source have great influence on the element content and internal structure of CDs rather than on the morphology of OSiCDs microspheres, which is probably the main reason for the redshift emission of OSiCDs microspheres. The ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy was further used to determine the ionization energy (IE) of OSiCDs microspheres (Fig. S4). The valence band maximum energy (Evbm) of m-SiCDs, o-SiCDs, and p-SiCDs microspheres is calculated to be −1.19, −1.92, and −2.37 eV (vs. Vacuum), respectively. And the electron onset energy (E0) of m-SiCDs, o-SiCDs and p-SiCDs microspheres is calculated to be 14.90, 14.43, and 14.62 eV, respectively. The IE of m-SiCDs, o-SiCDs, and p-SiCDs microspheres is calculated to be 5.13, 4.87, and 4.23 eV (IE = hv-(E0-Evbm)), respectively. The reduction of IE from m-SiCDs to p-SiCDs microspheres indicates that the electron in microspheres is easier to transition due to the increasing discrete energy level by nitrogen doping or conjugated system [44].

In order to further explore the fluorescence redshift mechanism of OSiCDs microspheres, fluorescence emission regularities of OSiCDs microspheres at different excitation wavelengths were studied. For m-SiCDs, the excitation spectra from 315 nm to 415 nm shows that the maximum fluorescence intensity appeared at 375 nm. And the emission peak position of m-SiCDs changes from 450 nm to 515 nm, which indicates that m-SiCDs display a slight excitation dependence (Fig. S5a). For o-SiCDs, the fluorescence intensity has a maximum at 465 nm in the excitation wavelength range of 365 nm–485 nm, indicating that the m-SiCDs display excitation independence (Fig. S5b). For p-SiCDs, the emission spectrum exhibits dual emission peak within the excitation wavelength range of 365–425 nm, an emission peak is only at 594 nm in the excitation wavelength range of 445–505 nm (Fig. S5c). From m-SiCDs to p-SiCDs, they exhibit different excitation dependence behavior, indicating that they have different fluorescence emission center [45]. Moreover, the average fluorescence lifetimes of the OSiCDs microspheres are 3.94 ns (m-SiCDs), 3.49 ns (o-SiCDs), and 2.79 ns (p-SiCDs), respectively, indicating that the OSiCDs microspheres’ lifetime decreases with their PL wavelength red shift. (Fig. S6). The lifetime decay of OSiCDs microspheres is related to the sp2 structure in luminescence core of CDs. The sp2 structure of CDs is more compact, the emission rate of radiant photo is quicker, and the lifetime decay is shorter [46]. The photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of OSiCDs microspheres was also measured. The PLQY of o-SiCDs is only 2.89 %, and the PLQY of m-SiCDs and p-SiCDs is relatively low, beyond the scope of instrument testing. Although the PLQYs of OSiCDs microspheres is relatively low, the fluorescence emission of OSiCDs microspheres can also be observed under ultraviolet light (Fig. 1b–d).

To explore the effect of CDs and organosilicon properties on the fluorescence phenomena of OSiCDs microspheres, they were etched with 2 mg/L NaOH solution. OSiCDs microspheres were soaked in 2 mg/L NaOH solution to etch at 60 °C for 48 h. It is obvious that the mixture is insoluble in solvent before etching, and it becomes clear after etching, which indicates that the organosilicon has been etched away to obtain a CDs solution (Fig. 3 a). In addition, the fluorescence of the three kinds of OSiCDs microspheres change before and after etching in NaOH solution. The emission spectra of the etching solution of OSiCDs microspheres were further studied at different excitation wavelengths, and it can be found that they have different degrees of excitation dependence (Fig. 3b–d). It proves that the phenylenediamine isomers formed CDs with different structures and fluorescence properties that would lead to different fluorescence properties of OSiCDs microspheres. Further comparative observation shows that the emission peak positions of OSiCDs microspheres occurs red shift than those of after etching by NaOH solution at the same excitation wavelength, which may be due to the resonance energy transfer (RET) effect between CDs in OSiCDs microspheres [47]. The above phenomena fully indicate that the CDs with blue, green, and red emission are achieved by the isomers of phenylenediamine as carbon sources, and then embedding them into the three-dimensional structure of organosilicon network to obtain different solid-state fluorescence OSiCDs microspheres.

Optical stability is an important indicator for the practical application of luminescent materials in fingerprint recognition [48]. Therefore, the thermostability, photobleaching resistance, and solvent resistance of OSiCDs microspheres were explored in Fig. 4 . OSiCDs microspheres were left at 30, 50, 70, 90, 110 and 130 °C for three days, and then their fluorescence intensity was obtained, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4a, their PL intensity remains basically stable at different treated temperatures. The OSiCDs microspheres were soaked separately in different organic solvents (bromobenzene, methanol, acetone, methylbenzene, tetrahydrofuran, 1,2-dichlorobenzene, chlorobenzene, and N, N-Dimethylformamide) for four days to test their fluorescence properties. As show in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of the OSiCDs microspheres varied less than 5 % without a change in the position of emission peak. The relative PL data of OSiCDs microspheres in Fig. 4c were collected under a 365 nm UV lamp for 8 days, respectively. The ratio of fluorescence intensity of OSiCDs microspheres to that of untreated samples fluctuates around 95 % and remains relatively stable within the error range, indicating that the OSiCDs microspheres have excellent photobleaching resistance. The above results can illustrate that the highly “Si-O-C″ and “Si-O-Si” cross-linked siloxane bonds network structure provides good protect to enhance the stability of OSiCDs microspheres.

Thanks to the superior morphology and fluorescent luminescence properties, OSiCDs microspheres may have great potential in the field of fingerprint recognition. A powder dusting operation is carried out to research OSiCDs microspheres in fingerprint recognition, includes three classic steps: fingermark printing, OSiCDs microspheres dusting, and fingermark development. Fig. 5 shows the fingerprint development effects of m-SiCDs, o-SiCDs, and p-SiCDs. OSiCDs microspheres selectively attach to the traces of LFPs, so patterns of the fingerprints are recognized by the luminescence exactly. The identification of level 1–3 features of LFPs is an important reference for confirming information in fingerprint detection. Using OSiCDs microspheres as developers, the level 1–3 features of LFPs on the aluminum foil paper substrate can be visible under 365 nm light. The large pictures on the left show level 1 patterns named left Loop. The ①, ②, and ③ images on the right are bifurcation, independent ridge, and beginnings, respectively, which belong to level 2 feature. Amazingly, level 3 patterns with sweat holes are even clearly formed by OSiCDs microspheres, showing additional detail points and individual features. For comparisons, the CDs were prepared only using the three isomers of phenylenediamine for fingerprint recognition (Fig. S7). It can be found that both the CDs solution and CDs powder cannot clearly identify the fingerprint. Thus, the reliability and accuracy of the three levels features of LFPs have been improved in forensic analysis through the use of OSiCDs microspheres.

As is well-known, LFPs may remain in a variety of complex crime scene environments, particularly left on substrates with different surfaces and colors. To further estimate the applicability of OSiCDs microspheres, several typical substrates were selected for fingerprint deposition, including glass, rigid plastics sheet, soft plastic bag, aluminized paper, blue paper packaging box, cigarette case and desktop. Generally, when the substrate surface is not smooth, the resolution of the visual fingerprint will be reduced [49]. In Fig. 6 , images of fingerprints developed with OSiCDs microspheres were taken by an iPhone 13 under a 365 nm UV lamp. It is evident that fingerprint patterns can be successfully presented on seven substrates surface. More importantly, extremely fine patterns, such as bifurcation and sweat hole, can also be acquired. Therefore, OSiCDs microspheres as the fingerprint developer have a wide range of applicability on different substrates, which can meet the needs of fingerprint identification to provide reliable evidence for identity authentication in a short time with minimum of facilities.

In order to explore the influence of substrate color on fingerprint development, m-SiCDs, o-SiCDs, and p-SiCDs were used to identify LFPs on red plastic. Compared with the development effect under LED light, the fluorescence properties of OSiCDs microspheres greatly improve the contrast of fingerprint patterns under 365 nm UV light (Fig. S8). However, p-SiCDs are not suitable for fingerprint recognition on the red substrate, because their fluorescence color is very close to the color of the substrate, leading to low contrast. But the red fluorescent of p-SiCDs played a significant fingerprint identification role on the paper cups with blue fluorescence (Fig. S9). o-SiCDs have a higher fluorescence intensity than m-SiCDs, so the fingerprint pattern recognized by o-SiCDs is clearer. Thus, as a fingerprint developer, the fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength of OSiCDs microspheres have a great effect on fingerprint development.

In summary, we successfully synthesized blue, yellow and red emissive OSiCDs microspheres by tuning three kinds isomers of phenylenediamine as carbon source in a simple one-step solvothermal method. The obtained OSiCDs microspheres have high dispersion and regular shape with a particle size of about 2 μm. It reveals that tunable solid-state fluorescence of OSiCDs microspheres is mainly attributed to the different fluorescent CDs form by different structure carbon source. Additionally, OSiCDs microspheres exhibit good fluorescence stability even in extreme environments such as high temperature, long time aging, and immersion in organic solvents. Moreover, the OSiCDs microspheres can identify level 1–3 features of LFPs on glass, aluminum foil, plastic, and other substrates. Notably, OSiCDs microspheres can still obtain clear fingerprint characteristics after the LFPs treated by ventilation or high temperature aging. Therefore, this work not only innovates a strategy for preparing multicolor solid-state fluorescent CDs composite, but also provides a variety of choices for LFPs recognition on different color substrates with high contrast and clarity.

Jiashi Chen: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Xiyue Cao: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Wen Fan: Software. Huijuan Ma: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Xuanfeng Jiang: Software, Formal analysis. Zhengguang Sun: Supervision, Formal analysis. Yuan Zhan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Our work is financially supported by the Foundation of Three Gorges Laboratory (SK232014).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.coco.2025.102277.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent nanomaterials for the development of latent fingerprints in forensic sciences
Adv. Funct. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Wang, M. Li, A. Yu, Y. Zhu, M. Yang, C. Mao, Fluorescent nanomaterials for the development of latent fingerprints in forensic sciences, Adv. Funct. Mater. 27 (2017) 1606243.
